subject: Chemistry
q: Carbylamine test is used to detect the presence of primary amino group in an organic compound. Which of the following compound is formed when this test is performed with aniline?
question: Carbylamine test is used to detect the presence of primary amino group in an organic compound. Which of the following compound is formed when this test is performed with aniline?
solution:
CARBYL amine given by 1° amine

Carbylamine reaction can be used to identify primary amino group in a given organic compound. When primary amines are treated with chloroform and alcoholic KOH, they give isocyanides (carbylamines) having very unpleasant smell which can be easily detected.

q: The increasing order of basicity of the following compounds is :
question: The increasing order of basicity of the following compounds is :
solution: The basic nature of amines can be explained on the basis hybridisation. The basic nature order is sp3>sp2>sp. If the lone pair on nitrogen involved in resonance, then it is less basic than others. In the molecules A, B and D, the nitrogen is sp2 hybridised. In the pyrrole molecule the lone pair on nitrogen involving in conjugation to get aromaticity. Hence, C is most basic and B is least basic.

question: An organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>11</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, shows optical activity and gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gas on treatment with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>HNO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo></math> reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PhSO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></math> producing a compound which is soluble in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>KOH</mi></math>. The structure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given:
1. Compound is optically active ⇒ contains chiral carbon/Nitrogen.
2. Compound liberates N2 gas with HNO2⇒ It is primary amine
3. Compound reacts with PhSO2Cl and the product is soluble in KOH - It is a primary amine.

Option (A) and (D) are primary amines. But only option (D) satisfy all the above conditions.

subject: Chemistry
q: A student has been given a compound " $x$ " of molecular formula- $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. ' $x$ ' is sparingly soluble in water. However, on addition of dilute mineral acid, ' $x$ ' becomes soluble in water. ' $x$ ' when treated with $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{KOH}($ alc $)$, ' $y$ ' is produced. ' y ' has a specific unpleasant smell. On treatment with benzenesulphonyl chloride, ' $x$ ' gives a compound ' $z$ ' which is soluble in alkali. The number of different " $H$ " atoms present in ' $z$ ' is:-
question: A student has been given a compound " $x$ " of molecular formula- $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. ' $x$ ' is sparingly soluble in water. However, on addition of dilute mineral acid, ' $x$ ' becomes soluble in water. ' $x$ ' when treated with $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{KOH}($ alc $)$, ' $y$ ' is produced. ' y ' has a specific unpleasant smell. On treatment with benzenesulphonyl chloride, ' $x$ ' gives a compound ' $z$ ' which is soluble in alkali. The number of different " $H$ " atoms present in ' $z$ ' is:-
solution:
Compound X has molecular formula $C_6H_7N$, is sparingly soluble in water but soluble in dilute mineral acid → primary aromatic amine (aniline).
X with $\text{CHCl}_3 + \text{KOH (alc.)}$ gives Y with unpleasant smell → carbylamine reaction, confirms primary amine.
X with benzenesulphonyl chloride gives Z which is soluble in alkali → Hinsberg test confirms primary amine. Z is N-phenylbenzenesulfonamide.
Structure of Z: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-NH-SO}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_5$

subject: Chemistry
q: The final product $[\mathrm{B}]$ is :
question: The final product $[\mathrm{B}]$ is :
solution:
Cyclohexylmethylamine reacts with benzoyl chloride under basic conditions to form amide [A]:
cyclohexylmethyl benzoamide.
LiAlH₄ reduces the amide carbonyl to CH₂, converting the N-benzoyl group to a benzyl group.
The product [B] is a secondary amine formed by reduction:
cyclohexylmethyl-benzylamine ($C_6H_{11}-CH_2-NH-CH_2-C_6H_5$).
This structure contains secondary amine (N-H) with two carbon substituents: cyclohexylmethyl and benzyl groups.

q:
Among the following structures, which will show the most stable enamine formation?

(Where Me is -CH3)
question:
Among the following structures, which will show the most stable enamine formation?

(Where Me is -CH3)
solution: All these enamines are interconvertible through their resonating structures. So most stable form is ‘C’ due to steric factor. All of these has the equal resonance only differ in their geometrical configuration. The enamine in option C has bulky groups on opposite side of double bond. means they have the minimum repulsion between them.
